full_text: Statement by the Delegations of the States Parties to the Treaty on the Establishment of the Union State of the Republic of Belarus and the Russian Federation at the Third Session of the Preparatory Committee for the 2026 Review Conference of the Parties to the Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons (New York, 28 April — 9 May 2025) Mr. Chair, Let me congratulate you on your election to this high office and assure you of the full support of the delegations of the Russian Federation and the Republic of Belarus.

We, the States Parties to the Treaty on the Establishment of the Union State of the Republic of Belarus and the Russian Federation, reaffirm our commitment to preserving and strengthening the nuclear non-proliferation regime and to its fundamental international instrument, the Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons (NPT).

It has been 55 years since the Treaty entered into force. During that period, the NPT has become an almost universal treaty and a cornerstone of the international peace and security, having demonstrated its relevance for the entire international community. We are convinced that preserving the NPT, ensuring its integrity while recognizing equal importance of all its provisions and all its three pillars — disarmament, nuclear non- proliferation and peaceful use of nuclear energy — is not merely a priority task for the NPT States Parties, but their core duty.

We have to state that at the moment, the processes intended to result in a nuclear- weapon-free world together with general and complete disarmament, have currently lost most of their momentum. Current international security and strategic stability situation objectively prevents any progress on this track. In our national and joint statements we have repeatedly pointed at the factors that have had severe negative influence on politico- military and strategic environment, creating major obstacles to both reaching and effectively implementing new agreements on disarmament initiatives. The factors with most destabilizing effect include NATO countries' hostile actions posing risk of a direct military confrontation between the nuclear powers, the preservation and further development of the arrangements and capabilities for the so-called 'nuclear sharing' by the North Atlantic Alliance, which has proclaimed itself to be a 'nuclear alliance', the

increasing scale of the US global missile defence accompanied by the build-up of counterforce capabilities for mounting disarming first strike, forward deployment of the US ground-based intermediate and shorter-range missiles; publicly announced US plans to place weapons in outer space.
